title: Competition for scarce resources
abstract: We model a downstream industry where firms compete to buy capacity in an upstream market which allocates capacity efficiently. Although downstream firms have symmetric production technologies, we show that industry structure is symmetric only if capacity is sufficiently scarce. Otherwise it is asymmetric, with one large, “fat,” capacity-hoarding firm and a fringe of smaller, “lean,” capacity-constrained firms. As demand varies, the industry switches between symmetric and asymmetric phases, generating predictions for firm size and costs across the business cycle. Surprisingly, increasing available capacity can cause a reduction in output and consumer surplus by resulting in such a switch.
summary_en: Consider an industry where downstream firms compete to buy capacity in an upstream market. The upstream market allocates capacity efficiently; the downstream firms are technologically symmetric. Yet this theory paper shows that the downstream industry structure is only symmetric when capacity is very scarce. Otherwise it splits into one large "fat" capacity-hoarding firm and a fringe of smaller "lean" capacity-constrained firms. As demand moves, the industry switches between regimes. A striking counter-intuitive implication: adding capacity can reduce total output and consumer surplus by flipping the market into the asymmetric regime.
policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian competition and infrastructure policy (GVH; energy and network regulators): expanding capacity in an input market is not unambiguously pro-competition — in some regions, capacity expansion can strengthen dominant downstream players at the expense of consumers. Applies to electricity generation, telecom spectrum, port capacity, rail slots. Use as a theoretical lens on capacity-allocation decisions rather than a quantitative model; empirical validation is setting-specific.
